text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultra-low anterior resection, followed by an intracorporeal coloanal stapled anastomosis, targeting the low rectal cancer.
Do we always need robotic surgery or transanal total mesorectal excision to achieve a good oncological and sphincter sparing dissection?
A seventy-one-year-old female with performance status zero presented with rectal bleeding.
The colonoscopy reveals a sessile polyp located four centimeters from the anal margin, visible within the rectal lumen.
Subsequent MRI scan was reported as T-III tumor with possible involvement of external sphincter, but no evidence of extramural invasion and no involved lymph nodes.
CT scan was negative for metastatic disease.
Interestingly, initial endoscopic biopsies showed high-grade dysplasia only.

The patient then had endoscopic ultrasound with repeat biopsy, which confirmed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma involving lateral wall but not extending to levator muscles or external sphincter.
The patient completed long-course chemoradiotherapy as per recommendation from the multidisciplinary team.
Repeat imaging confirmed good response to treatment with no convincing residual tumour on MRI and no metastatic disease on the CT scan.
Patient then proceeded to surgery.
After establishing pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon identifies and dissects the inferior mesenteric vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein near the ligament of Treitz, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the vein from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon creates a subvenous window following the division of the inferior mesenteric vein, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection in the caudal plane to identify the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully navigating through the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery near its origin from the abdominal aorta, completing the dissection of this major vessel.

For ultra-low rectal cancer located between five and three centimetres from the anal verge, like in this case, robotic ultra-low anterior resection or transanal total mesorectal excision are often advocated as preferred to the laparoscopic approach because of better views and feasibility of total mesorectal excision and intersphinteric dissection, with easier preservation of sphincter function and lower risk of positive circumferential margins.

However, unless in selected cases such as obese or male patients, a high oncological quality of resection with total mesorectal excision, safe radial margins and distal resection at the dentate line is still feasible laparoscopically, as shown here.

The surgeon performs careful transabdominal dissection in a craniocaudal direction, opening the mesorectal plane to mobilize the rectum and follow the intersphincteric plane, reaching the level of the dentate line.
The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to continue the surgical procedure, manipulating tissues within the mesorectal plane.
The surgeon palpates the tumor per rectum and marks the anal canal laparoscopically with a clip to ensure good macroscopic margins during division.
Following careful laparoscopic dissection in the intersphincteric plane, the surgeon divides the anal canal at or just above the dentate line, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.
The surgeon uses the previously placed clip to identify the tumor site, ensuring satisfactory macroscopic margins before dividing the anal canal.
The surgeon inserts the two arms of the flexible endostapler into the intersphincteric plane, aiming to reach the anal canal and secure adequate margins for resection.
The surgeon closes the flexible endostapler in the intersphincteric plane, maintaining its position to compress the tissues and ensure a secure seal before firing.
We suggest to extend the compression time beyond the recommendations of the manufacturer.
The surgeon waits for approximately ninety seconds with the stapler positioned in the intersphincteric plane before firing, ensuring adequate tissue compression for a secure seal.
The surgeon uses the flexible endostapler to compress and seal the thicker tissues of the anal canal, ensuring adequate margins for resection.
The video shows the pelvic floor after the division of the anal canal, with visible resection margins achieved using the flexible endostapler.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon and splenic flexure following the division of the anal canal, preparing these structures for further surgical manipulation.
The surgical specimen is delivered through a mini fan and steel incision approximately four to five centimeters long, created using a muscle-splitting technique.
The surgeon divides the descending colon, ensuring satisfactory perfusion of the remaining proximal colon, which is visibly assessed.
The surgeon secures the anvil of the circular stapler in the proximal end of the bowel, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon inserts the circular stapler transanally, carefully advancing the staple spike towards the midpoint of the staple line within the rectal cavity.
The surgeon encounters thicker and denser tissues near the anal margin, making the delivery of the circular stapler spike challenging in this region.
There is a considerable risk of tearing the staple line.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic right angle instrument to carefully manipulate tissues, minimizing the risk of damaging the staple line during the rectal cancer procedure.
Under direct vision, the surgeon secures the anvil to the spike of the circular stapler and closes the stapler, ensuring proper alignment and closure of the bowel ends.
The surgeon checks the orientation of the colon to ensure it is not twisted, confirming there is no tension on the anastomosis.
The surgeon holds the circular stapler closed for approximately ninety seconds, applying controlled compression to the rectal tissue before firing the device.
The surgeon focuses on achieving optimal tissue approximation and sealing of the staple line, ensuring the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon fires the stapler, then twists it to dislodge it from the anastomosis before removing it from the surgical site.
A closed suction Blake drain is placed below the pelvic brim in the retroanastomotic space, positioned to facilitate drainage from the surgical site.
This was removed on the sixth postoperative day.
A temporary diverting ileostomy is created in the right lower quadrant, with the stoma visibly positioned on the abdominal surface.

This case illustrates that in experienced hands laparoscopic approach to ultra-low anterior resection is feasible and allows intracorporeal ultra-low color anal anastomosis at the level of dentate line with total mesorectal excision.

If laparoscopic expertise is available, this top-down laparoscopic approach can be easier compared to intersphincteric dissection via perineal approach and is certainly more cost-effective than a robotic approach.
It is also minimally invasive and avoids the need for a permanent stoma or a perineal wound, decreasing the risk of infections and enhancing the recovery process.

Histology from the surgical specimen revealed residual small islands of viable moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma invading into muscularis propria with clear surgical margins and absent lymphovascular invasion.
Following uncomplicated post-op recovery, patient was discharged home on day eight.
